core_behavior.note: Model A mixes eventual and strong consistency, Model B focuses on validation/normalization/synchronization without specifying a consistency model, and Model C prescribes ACID, distributed coordination protocols, canonical sources, and explicit conflict resolution strategies — all representing different unspecified design choices; none of the models clearly resolves the strong vs eventual vs causal consistency model question uniformly.

inputs.note: Model A lists data transactions and configuration settings, Model B categorizes inputs by origin (user, external, system-generated), and Model C specifies technical inputs such as idempotency keys, vector clocks, CDC events, and schema migration scripts.

outputs.note: Model A emphasizes confirmation messages, alerts, and audit logs; Model B focuses on validated stored data and transmitted data; Model C specifies committed data state, acknowledgment responses, reconciliation reports, and conflict notifications.

assumptions.note: Model C has an empty assumptions array while Models A and B populate it with different design assumptions about storage architecture, consistency priority, and validation layers.

failure_modes.note: Model A specifies logging, admin notification, queuing during network partitions, and escalation to manual intervention; Model B specifies rejection with error messages, automated reconciliation, and fault-tolerant transaction techniques; Model C only lists operator alerts and conflict notifications without detailing recovery strategies.

edge_cases.note: Model A focuses on race conditions, migrations, third-party integrations, and stale caches; Model B addresses null inputs, duplicates, type mismatches, and concurrent updates; Model C focuses on stale data exposure, breaking schema changes, and idempotent duplicate submissions.

security_constraints.note: Model A restricts access to logs and mandates regulatory compliance; Model B adds encryption in transit and at rest, RBAC, and secure backups; Model C only specifies immutable audit log entries, omitting encryption and access control.

performance_constraints.note: Model B specifies explicit numeric thresholds (e.g., < 1 second processing, < 100 ms retrieval) while Models A and C specify no numeric thresholds, and Model C introduces a 24-hour idempotency key retention requirement not present in the others.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 3 of the 8 fields.
